question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a LIMIT clause in ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery to cause the DB to return all ethereum transactions matching a given timestamp and payerId, and if the DB has a bug or data anomaly where multiple rows match the same (consensus_timestamp, payer_account_id) pair, cause unbounded result set materialization that increases DB memory above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with transactionIds that all share the same payer_account_id but have slightly different valid_start_ns values, causing the DB to repeatedly scan the same index range for the same payer account and increasing DB buffer cache pressure above 30% of the 24-hour baseline through cache thrashing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() is called inside Promise.all() in getDetailedContractResults() alongside four other DB queries, so that a single crafted request triggers five simultaneous DB queries including the expensive ethereum_transaction JOIN, multiplying DB resource consumption and increasing it above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ORDER BY (result = successTransactionResult) DESC expression in transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery, which requires evaluating a boolean comparison against a constant for every row in the filtered set, to cause a CPU-intensive sort operation on a large intermediate result set and increase DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId() passes the raw nonce value directly as parameter $4 to the DB without any server-side validation or normalization, and by passing an extremely large nonce value, cause the DB to evaluate the coalesce($4, 0) subquery with an unexpected value that triggers a different query plan with higher resource consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() with a timestamp that corresponds to a block with many ethereum transactions, causing the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery to JOIN and return a large number of rows each containing multiple binary-encoded fields (signature_r, signature_s, chain_id, gas_price, etc.), increasing DB serialization CPU and network I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery uses a correlated subquery (SELECT coalesce($4, 0)) in the WHERE clause, which may be evaluated once per row by some DB query planners, causing O(n) subquery evaluations for n rows in the timestamp range and increasing DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that maxTransactionConsensusTimestampRangeNs is added to the validStartNs as a BigInt without any upper-bound clamping, so that for a validStartNs near the current time the range extends far into the future, causing the DB to scan all transactions from validStartNs to a future timestamp and increasing I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery selects the CALL_DATA column without any size restriction, and by identifying a known ethereum transaction with a very large call data payload (e.g., a contract deployment), trigger repeated fetches of that large blob to increase DB I/O and network bandwidth above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery does not use any covering index hint and selects consensus_timestamp, nonce, scheduled, type, payer_account_id from the transaction table, causing the DB to perform a heap fetch for each row in the index range scan and increasing random I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId() is called from both the /contracts/results/:transactionIdOrHash and /contracts/:contractId/results/:transactionIdOrHash endpoints, allowing a single attacker to target multiple API paths simultaneously to multiply the number of transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery executions and increase DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline without triggering per-endpoint rate limits?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() is called from getDetailedContractResults() which is itself called from multiple contract-related endpoints, allowing the attacker to trigger the same expensive ethereum_transaction JOIN from different API paths and increase aggregate DB resource consumption above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery uses AND valid_start_ns = $2 where $2 is the same value as the lower bound of the consensus_timestamp range, causing the DB to use a potentially suboptimal query plan that scans by consensus_timestamp range first before filtering by valid_start_ns, resulting in a larger intermediate result set and higher CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery has no LIMIT clause and the rows.map() call in getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId() creates a new EthereumTransaction object for every row, causing the Node.js garbage collector to work harder when many rows are returned and increasing Node.js process CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery is a static string built at class definition time and reused for every request, meaning there is no per-request query variation that would allow the DB to use different query plans, and by choosing inputs that consistently trigger the worst-case plan, sustain high DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId() calls getTransactionDetails() which calls super.getRows() which calls pool.queryQuietly(), and that queryQuietly() suppresses query errors silently, meaning a slow query that times out at the DB level may still hold a connection open and consume resources above 30% of the 24-hour baseline without surfacing an error to the caller?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery JOINs ethereum_transaction and transaction on both consensus_timestamp and payer_account_id, and that if the payer_account_id index on ethereum_transaction is not used (e.g., due to low selectivity), the DB performs a full scan of ethereum_transaction joined to transaction, increasing DB I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the nonce parameter defaults to undefined and is passed as $4 to the DB, causing the DB to use a different query plan for nonce=undefined vs nonce=0 vs nonce=N, and by alternating between these values, cause plan cache invalidation and increased query planning overhead that raises DB CPU above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getEthTransactionByTimestampAndPayerId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ethereumTransactionDetailsQuery selects the ACCESS_LIST column (a potentially large binary field representing EIP-2930 access lists) without any size limit, and by identifying ethereum transactions with large access lists, trigger repeated fetches that increase DB I/O and Node.js memory above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/transactionService.js -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: getTransactionDetailsFromTransactionId()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the transactionDetailsFromTransactionIdQuery selects from the transaction table without any partition pruning hint, and if the transaction table is partitioned by consensus_timestamp, a wide timestamp range spanning multiple partitions causes cross-partition scans that increase DB I/O above 30% of the 24-hour baseline?
